flash_cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: Conservation in the background of rapid decline in wildlife population and forestry has become essential.
cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India
Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: Conservation preserves the genetic diversity of plants and animals for better growth of species and breeding. For example, in agriculture, we are still dependent on traditional crop varieties.
cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India
Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: In the 1960s and 1970s, conservationists demanded a national wildlife protection programme.
cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: The Indian Wildlife (Protection) Act was implemented in 1972, with various provisions for protecting habitats. An all-India list of protected species was also published.
cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: The thrust of the programme was towards protecting the remaining population of certain endangered species.
cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India
Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: The central government has announced several projects for protecting specific animals which were greatly threatened.
cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: The projects include protection of animals such as the tiger, the one-horned rhinoceros, the Kashmir stag or hangul, the three types of crocodiles-the freshwater crocodile, the saltwater crocodile and the Gharial, the Asiatic lion and others.
cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: Most recently, the Indian elephant, black buck (chinkara), the great Indian bustard (godawan) and the snow leopard, etc. have been given full or partial legal protection against hunting and trade throughout India.
cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: The conservation projects are focusing on biodiversity rather than on a few of its components.
cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: Insects are included in the list of conservation planning.
cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: In the notification under Wildlife Act of 1980 and 1986, several hundred butterflies, moths, beetles, and one dragonfly have been added to the list of protected species.
cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: In 1991, for the first-time plants were added to the list of protected species, starting with six species.
cards.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

flash_cards.topic_name: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

cards.text: It is quite difficult to maintain, manage, and control vast forest and wildlife resources, even if citizens and government desire to conserve them.
cards.topic_name: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

cards.text: In India, much of its forest and wildlife resources are either owned or managed by the government through the Forest Department or other government departments.
cards.topic_name: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources
Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

cards.text:
Reserved Forest

  1. More than half of the total forest land has been declared reserved forests. 
  2. Reserved forests are regarded as the most valuable as far as the conservation of forest and wildlife resources are concerned.
cards.topic_name: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

cards.text:
Protected Forests 

  1. Almost one-third of the total forest area is protected forest, as declared by the Forest Department. 
  2. This forests land are protected from any further depletion.
cards.topic_name: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

cards.text:
Unclassed Forests
These are other forests and wastelands belonging to both government and private individuals and communities.
cards.topic_name: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources
Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources
Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources
Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources
Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

cards.text: Forests are home to some of the traditional communities, in some areas of India, local communities along with government officials are struggling their best to conserve the forests and habitats of wild life recognizing that only this will secure their own long term livelihood.
cards.topic_name: Community and Conservation

cards.text: In Sariska Tiger Reserve in Rajasthan, villagers have fought against mining by citing the Wildlife Protection Act.
cards.topic_name: Community and Conservation

cards.text: In many areas, villagers themselves are protecting habitats and rejecting any government involvement.
cards.topic_name: Community and Conservation

cards.text: The inhabitants of five villages in the Alwar district of Rajasthan have declared 1,200 hectares of forest as the Bhairodev Dakav ‘Sonchuri’, declaring their own set of rules and regulations which do not allow hunting and are protecting the wildlife against any outside encroachments.
cards.topic_name: Community and Conservation
Community and Conservation
